data.sentence: Okay, I think everyone's on — Diana, Marcus, you both there?
Yeah I'm here, I've been here for like five minutes actually, I was waiting on the bridge code to work.
Sorry, yeah the — the conferencing system was being weird, I don't know. Marcus?
Present. I've got the incident channel pulled up and I'm looking at the logs right now.
Okay good. So — Diana, before you say anything, I want to give you a status update first so we're all working from the same picture, okay?
Tyler I have eight customers blowing up my phone right now, so please, go ahead, talk fast.

Right. So — so the event processing pipeline went down at approximately 2:47 AM this morning. We're seeing a cascading failure that started in the ingestion layer and basically knocked out the entire threat monitoring feed for Detect. Nobody is getting alerts right now.
Nobody is — okay so you're telling me that every single Detect customer is flying blind right now? Like zero visibility?
That is correct, yes. Zero event processing, zero alerts going out.
Tyler, this is — this is really bad. Do you understand how bad this is? We have enterprise customers who have contractual SLA commitments around monitoring uptime.
I know, Diana, I know. We're — we are working it.

Can I jump in here? So from a support standpoint, I've already had, uh, eleven tickets come in since about 6 AM when people started getting to work and noticed their dashboards were blank. Hartwell Financial, Crestwood Health, um, Meridian Logistics — all of them are asking for ETAs and I have nothing to give them.
Okay so Marcus what are you telling them right now? What's the messaging?
I'm telling them we're aware of a service disruption affecting Detect and that our engineering team is actively investigating. That's... that's basically all I have.
That is not good enough, that is not — Tyler, can you give me something more concrete? What is the root cause, do we actually know?

We think — and I want to be careful here because we're still digging — but we think it's a single point of failure in the ingestion layer. Basically one node failed and instead of the load distributing properly it just, uh, it cascaded. Took everything downstream with it.
A single point of failure? We have a single point of failure in production in a threat monitoring pipeline? How is that even — how did that get through architecture review?
Diana, I — look, I hear you and honestly I'm asking myself the same question right now but that is a conversation for after we fix it, okay? Can we focus?
No, I'm sorry, I'm not letting that go because I need to understand this for when I talk to customers. Hartwell's CISO is going to ask me that exact question.
Then tell him we identified an architectural gap in our event ingestion redundancy and we are remediating it. That's the honest answer and it's all we have right now.
I need an ETA Tyler. What is your best estimate for restoration?

Uh — okay so honestly, we're looking at somewhere between four and eight hours to get the pipeline stable again. We need to stand up redundant nodes and we don't want to rush it because if we botch the recovery we could lose event data on top of everything else.
Four to eight hours. Tyler, it's already been — what, almost four hours since 2:47? So we could be looking at twelve hours total of zero threat monitoring visibility.
In a worst case scenario, yes. I'm not going to sugarcoat it.
I've got — okay, I just got another ticket while we've been talking. Crestwood Health is saying they're, quote, evaluating alternative vendors. They're asking about SentinelShield.
Of course they are. Of course. Marcus, flag that one to me directly, I'm going to call Crestwood's account lead personally.
Yeah, flagging it now.

Diana I want to — I want to propose something. Can we do thirty-minute status update emails going out to all affected customers? Marcus coordinates, engineering feeds me the latest, and we keep a consistent message. No more vacuum.
Yes. Yes, that is the bare minimum we should be doing, why is that not already happening?
Because we've been triaging the actual technical failure, Diana, we don't have unlimited people here.
Marcus, can you own the customer comms cadence? I'll draft the first email template right now and get it to you in the next fifteen minutes.
Yeah, I can do that. Should I loop in the CSMs for their specific accounts or keep it centralized?
Loop them in but all comms go through you so we don't have five different people saying five different things. One voice.
Got it.
Tyler — and I'm asking this seriously, not to be combative — do we need to escalate this to the executive team? Like does Raj need to know about this right now?
I already pinged Raj at 4 AM. He's aware. He's not on this bridge because we told him we'd update him at noon with status.

Okay. Okay good. Alright — so Tyler, your team is on remediation, you will feed Marcus updates every thirty minutes, Marcus coordinates customer comms, I'm going to personally call Crestwood and Hartwell in the next hour. Does that — does that cover the immediate next steps?
That covers it. And Diana — for what it's worth, I'm sorry. This should not have happened and I know that doesn't fix anything but... we are going to get it fixed.

I know you are. I know. But Tyler, when this is over we are having a very long conversation about redundancy architecture because I cannot — I cannot go back to customers with something like this again. I just can't.
Agreed. That conversation is going to happen, I promise. Okay — I need to get back to the incident. Marcus, I'll send you the first update by 10:30.
I'll be watching for it. Alright, let's go.
